summary: Service sequence after Torah reading: five immersions and ten sanctifications of the HP. Vestment changes between white linen and golden vestments. The kiyor used for all ten sanctifications.

display.whats: The Mishnah's service sequence for the HP after the Torah reading: he went to the bull and goat, performed the day's service with the five immersions and ten sanctifications. The Gemara analyzes the order in detail.
display.hint: Why did the HP need five immersions during the Yom Kippur service?
display.title: Five Immersions, Ten Sanctifications: The HP's Ritual Architecture

learning.ahaMoment: Five immersions and ten sanctifications correspond to five vestment changes: the HP alternated between white linen (inner service) and gold (outer service) five times during the day. Each change required immersion and sanctification of hands and feet.
learning.memoryAnchor: Five immersions, ten sanctifications - the HP changed clothes (and immersed) five times throughout Yom Kippur.
learning.learnerQuestion: How many times did the HP immerse and sanctify his hands and feet on Yom Kippur, and what triggered each round of purification?
learning.coreTension: The HP changed vestments multiple times. Each change required ritual preparation. Is this repeated purification purely formalistic, or does each immersion serve a distinct purpose?
learning.coreMove: The HP immersed five times and sanctified his hands and feet ten times (twice per change - before and after each vestment transition). Five vestment changes between white linen vestments (for inner service) and golden vestments (for outer service) each required their own purification cycle.

takeaway.text: Five vestment changes, five immersions, ten sanctifications - the repeated ritual preparations mark each transition as a new beginning, ensuring the HP enters each service mode with full purity.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may assume the HP immersed once at the start of the day. The repeated immersions are triggered by the vestment changes, not by time - each transition between white and golden vestments requires its own purification.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Service order after Torah reading
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: After the Torah reading: rams (HP's ram and people's ram) and sin-offering fats at the third immersion; afternoon tamid at the fifth immersion.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Ladle and fire-pan removal between rams and mussafin
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The removal of the ladle and fire-pan from the Holy of Holies comes after the rams and sin-offering fats and before the mussafin - as derived from the passage sequence.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: He performed as written - Torat Kohanim precedes Numbers
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The service follows scripture: Torat Kohanim (Lev.) is written first, so the day's service precedes the mussafin of Numbers.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: External goat atones for same category as internal
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The external mussaf goat atones for the same category of sin as the internal Yom Kippur goat - sins with awareness at the end but not at the beginning, as derived in Shevuot.

halachic: five immersions and ten sanctifications of HP
service sequence from Lev. and Num.
narrative: the HP's full day of service structured around immersions

misconceptions.misconception: The HP only immersed once during the Yom Kippur service
misconceptions.correction: The HP immersed five times and sanctified hands and feet ten times - at each vestment change between white linen (inner) and golden (outer) vestments.

visualizableElements.description: Five immersions: (1) first service - white; (2) bull burning - gold; (3) rams + eimurim - white; (4) ladle and fire-pan - white; (5) afternoon tamid + mussafin - gold

quizSeeds.question: How many times did the HP immerse on Yom Kippur and why?
quizSeeds.answer: Five times - corresponding to five vestment changes between white linen vestments (for inner service) and golden vestments (for outer service). Each change required immersion and sanctification of hands and feet.

sugyot.finalRuling: The HP immersed five times and sanctified ten times. Service follows Torat Kohanim sequence: day's service first, then rams and eimurim, then ladle and fire-pan, then mussafin and afternoon tamid.

display.whats: The Gemara discusses the kiyor (laver): when the HP sanctified his hands and feet from the kiyor, he sat rather than stood. Discussion of the kiyor's use in the five sanctification sequences.
display.hint: Why is the HP permitted to sit for sanctification from the kiyor when normally all Temple service must be done standing?
display.title: The Kiyor and the Golden Stool: Sanctification's Special Setting

learning.ahaMoment: The HP's sitting by the kiyor is not about comfort but about practical necessity: the kiyor was positioned at a height requiring a specific posture. The permission is carefully circumscribed.
learning.memoryAnchor: HP sat on golden stool by the kiyor - sanctification on Yom Kippur had its own special rules.
learning.learnerQuestion: Why was a golden stool provided for the HP at the kiyor during Yom Kippur sanctification, and what does this setting signal?
learning.coreTension: The kiyor is a functional ritual vessel. Why is special furniture needed alongside it? Is the golden stool purely about comfort or does it carry ceremonial significance?
learning.coreMove: A golden stool was provided at the kiyor to support the HP during sanctification. This is not merely about comfort - the dignified setting honors the sanctification act itself. The HP alternated five times between white linen (Holy of Holies) and golden (outer service) vestments, each requiring immersion and sanctification.

takeaway.text: The golden stool at the kiyor dignifies even the preparation acts - sanctification is performed with kavod appropriate to the occasion, not merely as a functional prerequisite to service.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may see the kiyor as merely functional. The golden stool signals that even preparatory acts carry their own ceremonial weight and are performed with honor befitting the occasion.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: HP used kiyor for all ten sanctifications
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: The HP sanctified hands and feet from the kiyor ten times - twice for each of the five immersions.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Sat on golden stool for kiyor sanctification
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: For the kiyor sanctification on Yom Kippur, the HP sat on a golden stool - a special accommodation for the HP's repeated use throughout the day.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: First immersion sanctification at the kiyor
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The first immersion sequence: the HP immersed, dried off, dressed in white linen, then sanctified hands and feet at the kiyor before beginning the morning tamid and day's service.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Service sequence for each vestment change
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Each of the five vestment changes follows the same pattern: immersion, drying, dressing, sanctification at the kiyor, then service.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Golden vestments for outer service
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The HP wore golden vestments for the outer (non-Holy of Holies) services - for the morning and afternoon tamid, mussafin, and other external services.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: White linen for inner service
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The HP wore white linen vestments for all inner (Holy of Holies) services - the blood sprinklings, incense offering, and removal of ladle and fire-pan.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Vestment order follows the five immersion sequence
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The complete sequence: (1) white - morning tamid and day's service; (2) gold - burning bull carcass; (3) white - rams and eimurim; (4) white - ladle and fire-pan; (5) gold - afternoon tamid and mussafin.

halachic: golden stool for HP at kiyor
theological: alternation between white (inner) and gold (outer) as spiritual rhythm
narrative: the HP's physical day - five immersions, ten sanctifications, multiple changes

misconceptions.misconception: The HP wore the same vestments throughout Yom Kippur
misconceptions.correction: He changed vestments five times - alternating between white linen (for the Holy of Holies) and golden (for outer service), immersing and sanctifying at each change.

visualizableElements.description: Five vestment changes: white-gold-white-white-gold, with immersion and kiyor sanctification at each transition

quizSeeds.question: How many times did the HP change his vestments on Yom Kippur?
quizSeeds.answer: Five times - alternating between white linen (for inner/Holy of Holies service) and golden vestments (for outer/external service), immersing each time.

sugyot.finalRuling: The HP sanctified hands and feet from the kiyor ten times (twice per vestment change). He changed between white and gold vestments five times with immersion and drying at each change.

display.whats: The HP used the kiyor for sanctification between each service segment. The Gemara notes the kiyor's role as the sanctification vessel throughout the day.
display.hint: What is the significance of the kiyor being specifically the vessel for the HP's ten sanctifications?
display.title: The Kiyor as Crossing Point: Each Sanctification Marks a Transition

learning.ahaMoment: The kiyor is literally the 'crossing point' between service segments - each sanctification is both a conclusion of one service and a preparation for the next.
learning.memoryAnchor: Kiyor = the transition point of Yom Kippur. Each sanctification closes one chapter and opens the next.
learning.learnerQuestion: Why does the HP sanctify his hands and feet ten times - twice for each of five vestment changes - and what does the kiyor represent as this transition point?
learning.coreTension: Five vestment changes would seem to require five sanctifications. Why does the count reach ten? And why is the kiyor specifically required rather than any vessel of water?
learning.coreMove: Each vestment change requires sanctification both before entering and before exiting each service mode - hence ten times from five changes. Only the kiyor (Temple laver) is valid for this sanctification. The kiyor is the literal pivot point of the Yom Kippur service: every transition passes through it.

takeaway.text: Ten sanctifications (twice per vestment change) because each transition requires separate preparation for entering and exiting. The kiyor alone is valid - making it the service's mandatory crossing point.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may assume sanctification is required once per vestment set. The Gemara establishes it is required at each entry and each exit - ten times total, and only the kiyor qualifies.

argumentFlow.label: Ruling: Kiyor used for all ten sanctifications of Yom Kippur
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The kiyor is the designated vessel for all ten sanctifications of the HP on Yom Kippur - hands and feet together, before each service segment after each vestment change.

halachic: kiyor as vessel for ten sanctifications
theological: sanctification as spiritual preparation for service
narrative: the HP returning to the kiyor ten times throughout the day

misconceptions.misconception: Any vessel of water could be used for the HP's Yom Kippur sanctification
misconceptions.correction: Only the kiyor (Temple laver) is valid for sanctification of hands and feet in Temple service.

visualizableElements.description: HP approaching the kiyor ten times throughout the day - hands and feet, before each service

quizSeeds.question: How many times did the HP sanctify his hands and feet from the kiyor on Yom Kippur?
quizSeeds.answer: Ten times - twice for each of the five vestment changes (once before and once after each change).

sugyot.finalRuling: The HP sanctified from the kiyor ten times on Yom Kippur.

rashiTranslations.he: ואחר כך אילו ואיל העם ואח"כ אימורי חטאת. כל אלו בטבילה
rashiTranslations.en: And then the HP's ram and the people's ram and then the sin-offering fats - all these at the third immersion.

rashiTranslations.he: שלישית: ואחר כך תמיד של בין הערבים. בטבילה חמישית ולא
rashiTranslations.en: And then the afternoon tamid - at the fifth immersion. We do not discuss here the removal of the ladle and fire-pan.

rashiTranslations.he: איירי הכא בהוצאת כף ומחתה דפשיטא לן דבתר אילו ואיל העם
rashiTranslations.en: Since it is obvious to us that it comes after the rams, as taught later: the entire portion was stated in order.

rashiTranslations.he: היא כדתניא לקמן שכל הפרשה נאמרה על הסדר וכו': עביד
rashiTranslations.en: He performed as written - Torat Kohanim was written first.

rashiTranslations.he: כדכתיב. תורת כהנים כתיבא
rashiTranslations.en: Therefore the rams and sin-offering fats come after the day's service, then removal of ladle and fire-pan.

rashiTranslations.he: ברישא הלכך אילו ואיל העם ואימורי
rashiTranslations.en: Then the rams and the sin-offering fats and then the ladle and fire-pan.

rashiTranslations.he: חטאת בתר עבודת היום והדר הוצאת
rashiTranslations.en: Then the mussafin and afternoon tamid which are written last in Numbers.

rashiTranslations.he: כף ומחתה ואחר כך מוספין ותמיד
rashiTranslations.en: This 'besides the sin-offering of atonement' - which implies the external goat precedes the rams.

rashiTranslations.he: של בין הערבים דכתיבי לבסוף
rashiTranslations.en: What does he do with it? Granted 'besides the morning burnt-offering' is not written for Yom Kippur itself.

rashiTranslations.he: בחומש הפקודים: האי מלבד חטאת
rashiTranslations.en: And the continuum-learner does not derive from it to do the Torat Kohanim things first.

rashiTranslations.he: הכפורים. דמשמע דשעיר החיצון
rashiTranslations.en: But this 'besides the sin-offering of atonement' written for Yom Kippur - what does he do with it?

rashiTranslations.he: קודם לאילו ואיל העם מאי עביד ליה
rashiTranslations.en: What the internal goat atones for, the external goat also atones for - as derived in the first chapter of Shevuot.

rashiTranslations.he: בשלמא מלבד עולת הבקר לאו ביום
rashiTranslations.en: For awareness at the end without awareness at the beginning - the external goat atones.

rashiTranslations.he: הכיפורים כתיב ומרגלים לא יליף
rashiTranslations.en: As it says: 'a sin-offering goat, besides the sin-offering of atonement' - what the one atones for the other atones for.

rashiTranslations.he: דסמיך אסידרא למיעבד הני דתורת
rashiTranslations.en: Just as the internal goat does not atone except for something with awareness at the end.

rashiTranslations.he: כהנים ברישא אלא האי דכתב ביום
rashiTranslations.en: As explained there, so too the external goat does not atone, etc.

rashiTranslations.he: הכפורים גופיה מאי עביד ליה:
rashiTranslations.en: In his name - in Rabbi Akiva's name.

rashiTranslations.he: על מה שזה מכפר וכו'. במתניתין
rashiTranslations.en: One - of the seven lambs.

rashiTranslations.he: דפ"ק דשבועות (דף ב.) דריש ליה
rashiTranslations.en: He performed one - as written 'besides the morning burnt-offering' to link some of the mussafin to the morning tamid.

rashiTranslations.he: הכי ועל שיש בה ידיעה בסוף ואין
rashiTranslations.en: And then he performed the day's service - for if you say all mussafin come with the morning tamid.

rashiTranslations.he: בה ידיעה בתחילה שעיר הנעשה
rashiTranslations.en: Perhaps he will become tired and be unable to perform the day's service which is the essential atonement.

rashiTranslations.he: בחוץ מכפר שנאמר ושעיר חטאת
rashiTranslations.en: Perhaps he will neglect - and not do them after the day's service.

rashiTranslations.he: אחד מלבד חטאת הכפורים על
rashiTranslations.en: And if you worry he might become tired - there is no concern.

rashiTranslations.he: מה שזה מכפר זה מכפר מה פנימי
rashiTranslations.en: For regarding the day's service he is energetic and regains his strength.

rashiTranslations.he: אינו מכפר אלא על דבר שיש בו
rashiTranslations.en: And all the mussafin he did not perform - for Torat Kohanim precedes and he needs to fulfill both this and that sequence.

rashiTranslations.he: ידיעה כדמפרש התם אף חיצון
rashiTranslations.en: All opinions hold - both Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Akiva.

rashiTranslations.he: אינו מכפר וכו': משמו. של ר'
rashiTranslations.en: One ram is for the community - for all say 'the people's ram' and they do not discuss the ram written in Numbers at all.

rashiTranslations.he: עקיבא: אחד. מן שבעת כבשים:
rashiTranslations.en: The ram mentioned here - in Torat Kohanim: 'and one ram for a burnt-offering.'

rashiTranslations.he: עביד חד. כדכתיב מלבד עולת
rashiTranslations.en: And they are both needed - there is reason to think a freewill offering is better to be chosen so it will be accepted willingly.

rashiTranslations.he: הבקר לסמוך מקצת המוספין לעולת
rashiTranslations.en: And there is reason to think an obligatory offering is better to be choice so he fulfills his obligation properly.

rashiTranslations.he: התמיד: והדר עביד עבודת היום.
rashiTranslations.en: As taught.

rashiTranslations.he: דאי אמרת כל המוספין יקריב עם
rashiTranslations.en: And then the rams and the people's ram and then the sin-offering fats - all in the third immersion.

rashiTranslations.he: תמיד של שחר דילמא חליש ולא
rashiTranslations.en: For the text of Torat Kohanim is written first, therefore the rams and sin-offering fats come after the day's service.

rashiTranslations.he: מצי למיעבד עבודת היום והוא
rashiTranslations.en: And then the ladle and fire-pan removal and then the mussafin and afternoon tamid.

rashiTranslations.he: עיקר הכפרה: דילמא פשע. ולא
rashiTranslations.en: This 'besides the sin-offering of atonement' which implies the external goat precedes the rams.

rashiTranslations.he: עביד להו אחר עבודת היום ולמאי
rashiTranslations.en: What does he do with it? Granted 'besides the morning burnt-offering' is not written for Yom Kippur.

rashiTranslations.he: דחיישת דילמא חליש ליכא למיחש
rashiTranslations.en: But 'besides the sin-offering of atonement' written for Yom Kippur itself - what does he do with it?

rashiTranslations.he: דלגבי עבודת היום זריז הוא ומחליף
rashiTranslations.en: On what the one atones the other atones - as derived in the first chapter of Shevuot.

rashiTranslations.he: כח וכולהו מוספין לא עביד דתורת
rashiTranslations.en: From his name - Rabbi Akiva's name. One - of the seven lambs.

rashiTranslations.he: כהנים קדים וצריך לקיים זו וזו סדר
rashiTranslations.en: He performed one - as written 'besides the morning burnt-offering' to attach some mussafin to the morning tamid.

rashiTranslations.he: הכתוב ומדרשי המקראות: דכולי
rashiTranslations.en: And then the day's service.

rashiTranslations.he: עלמא מיהא. בין לר' אליעזר בין
rashiTranslations.en: For if you say all mussafin with the morning tamid, perhaps he will tire and be unable to perform the day's service.

rashiTranslations.he: לר' עקיבא חד איל הוא דציבור
rashiTranslations.en: Perhaps he will neglect - and not do them after. And if you worry about tiredness - no concern.

rashiTranslations.he: דכולהו אמרי איל העם ולא איירי
rashiTranslations.en: For the day's service he is energetic and regains strength. And all mussafin he did not do for Torat Kohanim precedes.

rashiTranslations.he: באיל הכתוב בחומש הפקודים כלל:
rashiTranslations.en: All opinions hold. One ram is for the community.

rashiTranslations.he: האמור כאן. בתורת כהנים ואיל אחד לעולה: וצריכי. שיש
rashiTranslations.en: The ram mentioned here - in Torat Kohanim: and one ram as a burnt-offering.

rashiTranslations.he: דעת נוטה לומר נדבה מובחרת כדי שתהא מקובלת ברצון ויש
rashiTranslations.en: And they are both needed - reason for freewill offering quality; reason for obligatory offering quality.

rashiTranslations.he: דעת נוטה לומר חובה תהא מובחרת כדי שיצא ידי חובתו יפה:
rashiTranslations.en: As the entire.

rashiTranslations.he: שכל
rashiTranslations.en: All opinions.
